text:
This article examines the current crisis in Youth Ministry through the lens of Reformed theology’s *Sola Scriptura* principle. Despite various programmatic efforts, contemporary Youth Ministry often fails to guide young people toward sustainable Christian maturity. A primary driver of this inefficiency is the systemic isolation of adolescents from the adult world, particularly from their own parents and the broader intergenerational church community.

Drawing on biblical foundations, this study proposes a shift toward a family-orientated approach to Youth Ministry. Within this framework, parents are recognized as the primary mediators of their children’s spiritual development, while the church serves as a vital support system to empower families. By identifying the specific roles of parents, youth workers, and the congregation, the article offers fresh perspectives on how these stakeholders can move beyond autonomous, segregated programming toward a more integrated, effective model of discipleship.
